html:
VIII

It was near noontide, and the heat greater and greater. The people were all assembled outside the District Office; but the head of the District had not yet appeared. The scrivener had several times come out upon the threshold and, shading his eyes, looked down the broad highway, with its borders of gnarled willow-trees. Nothing was visible but the glittering pools which yesterdayвАЩs shower had leftвБ†вАФone cart crawling along, and a peasantвАЩs white capote fluttering among the trees.

So they waited patiently. The Voyt alone rushed bustling to and fro, restless and fidgety, now looking out upon the road, now urging forward the work of the men who were filling up the hollows in the square before the Office.

вАЬFaster, lads! faster, for GodвАЩs sake! He will be here ere ye have done the work!вАЭ

A voice from the crowd called out: вАЬBeware lest ye be so scared that some accident happen to you!вАЭ

вАЬNow, men, stir yourselves! I am here on duty: such jests are untimely.вАЭ

вАЬOur Voyt, вАЩtis known, fears God alone!вАЭ said one peasant, a man from Rzepki.

The Voyt, now furious, shrieked: вАЬIf any man speak one word more, IвАЩll have him thrown in jail!вАЭвБ†вАФAnd then he ran round to the cemetery that stood upon the height on which the District Office was perched.

It was overshadowed by many an ancient tree, through whose branches the grey church tower was seen; the black arms of the crosses bent over the stone wall, and above the road that led through the village.

Nothing was to be seen as yet. The Voyt left the Soltys along with the people, and went into the Office. Here someone was continually entering, called in by the scrivener, who took occasion to remind him gently of taxes in arrears, unpaid subscriptions for the court buildingsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and other things still more important. These reminders were truly very distasteful to each of them: how could they pay in such hard times, and just before the harvest? So they only made him a very deep bow, some even kissing his hand, and some pressing their last zloty into the manвАЩs outstretched palm. But they all implored him to wait till the harvest, or till next fair.

That scrivener! he was a cunning blade, a wily crafty old fox! How many a way he had of fleecing the people! To some he would make no end of promises, others he wrought upon through fear of the gendarmes; these he got the upper hand of through sheer flattery, and those, by treating them with free and easy friendliness. But he always and somehow got something out of every one of them. He was in need of oats, or he required a few young goslings for the head of the District; or he obtained a promise of some straw ropes for binding sheaves. And, willingly or unwillingly, they promised whatever he wanted. And thenвБ†вАФjust as they were leavingвБ†вАФhe would take those apart whom he knew best, and say to them in friendly guise:

вАЬLook ye, vote for the school; for if ye should oppose it, our head may wax angry, and peradventure cancel your agreement with the Squire as to the forest.вАЭ

вАЬHowвАЩs that?вАЭ cried Ploshka, in astonishment. вАЬWhy, we made the agreement freely on either side.вАЭ

вАЬAye, but know ye not?вБ†вАФвАШBut noble with noble is hand in glove; for peasants, never noble has love.вАЩвАКвАЭ

Much dismayed, Ploshka left him; and he continued to call the men in, frightening each of them in a different way, but pressing them all to do the same thing.

A good many people were gathered togetherвБ†вАФmore than two hundredвБ†вАФwho at first grouped themselves by villages, each with his own acquaintances: men of Lipka with men of Lipka, and so on. But now it was known to be the head of the DistrictвАЩs will they should vote about the school, they began to mingle together, passing from group to group as it suited them. Only the вАЬnobilityвАЭ of Rzepki held proudly aloof, looking down upon the other peasantry. All the rest had presently mixed together, like lentils in a dish, all over the square, but congregating mostly in the shadow of the churchyard trees, or about the wagons.

But it was round the large tavern that they thronged closest. This stood opposite the District Office, surrounded by a clump of trees, as in a shady grove; and many a one went that way to refresh himself with a glass of beer, after standing so long in the hot glare. The tavern being chock-full, quite a number of groups were lounging about under the trees, discussing the news and attentively watching both the Office and the other side of the house, where the scrivener lived, and where the noise and bustle was greatest.

From time to time, the scrivenerвАЩs wife thrust her fat face out of a back window, screaming:

вАЬMake haste, Magda! O you sluggard! may you break both your legs!вАЭ

The girl was heard every now and then rushing about the rooms, the panes quivering to her tread; a child would squall with shrill vehemence; somewhere behind, the fowls were cackling in great trepidation, and a panting constable was hunting chickens in the corn and down the road.

вАЬBelike they are going to feast the head official,вАЭ someone remarked.

вАЬThey say the scrivener brought in half a cartload of liquor yesterday.вАЭ

вАЬThen theyвАЩll get as drunk as they did last year.вАЭ

вАЬOh, they can afford it. Do not the people pay, and is there anyone to watch what their hands grab?вАЭ said Matthew: to whom another cried at once:

вАЬBe silent! the gendarmes have come.вАЭ

вАЬThey prowl about like wolves: where they go, and by which ways, who can tell?вАЭ

So they stood mute with fear, when the gendarmes drew up in a line before the Office, with a number of people round them: amongst whom were conspicuous the miller, the Voyt, andвБ†вАФat a little distanceвБ†вАФthe blacksmith, alert and attentive.

вАЬThat miller!вБ†вАФHe fawns upon them, like a famished dog!вАЭ

вАЬWherever the gendarmes are seen, look out for the District Official!вАЭ Gregory exclaimed, passing over to where Antek, Matthew, Klemba, and Staho were talking together. Then they parted to mix with the people, holding forth and expressing their opinions with much force. They were listened to in silence; sometimes one or another of their hearers would groan and scratch his head with an embarrassed air, or cast a glance at the gendarmes, now drawing closer to one another.

Antek, with his back against the corner of the tavern, spoke curtly, but with conviction, and an air of authority. In another group, Matthew was talking humorously and making many a man laugh at his jests, while in a third crowd, nearer the cemetery, Gregory lectured with much ability, and as if he were reading out of an open book!

But their speeches all tended in one direction: to oppose the head official, to vote against the school, and not to heed those who were always on the side of the officials.

No one else uttered a word, but all nodded assent: even the greatest fools among them knew well that such a school meant nothing but the payment of new taxes for nothing: which no one cared for.

The multitude, however, were restless, shifting uneasily from one foot to the other, coughing and clearing their throats.вБ†вАФThey were terribly afraid of opposing the head official and his satellites.

One man looked at another, secretly troubled what to do; and everyone noted carefully what the richest among them seemed to think. As to the miller and the foremost men in the other villages, they appeared to put themselves forward on purpose to be favorably noticed by the gendarmes and the scrivener.

Antek went to speak with them; but the miller said rudely: вАЬAny man but a fool can tell how he should vote!вАЭ and turned to the blacksmith, who was of everybodyвАЩs opinion, and always gliding about from group to group, guessing shrewdly how matters would turn out. He talked to the scrivener, chatted with the miller, offered Gregory a pinch of snuffвБ†вАФand kept his own counsel meanwhile so well that to the very end no one knew on which side he was.

The majority were meanwhile gradually inclining not to vote for the school. They now dispersed about the square, indifferent to the noonday heat, and were setting to canvass their views still louder and more boldly than before, when the scrivener called from the open window:

вАЬHere, someone of you!вАЭ

No one stirred.

вАЬLet someone run over to the Manor for the fish. вАЩTwas to be brought here in the morning, and we are waiting still.вБ†вАФCome!вАЭ he shouted masterfully; вАЬmake haste!вАЭ

Here a voice uttered the bold words:

вАЬWe are not here as your servants!вАЭ

вАЬLet him run thither himself! It irks him to drag his paunch about!вАЭ At this they laughed, for his belly was indeed as big as a drum.

The scrivener swore. But in a minute, out came the Voyt at the back of the house, who, passing behind the tavern, slipped away to the Manor by the outside of the village.

вАЬHe must have been changing the clothes of the babies at Madam ScrivenerвАЩs, and cleaning them likewise: so he has gone out for a little fresh air.вАЭ

вАЬAh, yes; Madam likes not her rooms to be noisome.вАЭ

вАЬShe will soon find other services for him to render her.вАЭ

вАЬStrange that the Squire is not yet to be seen,вАЭ they said in some surprise; but the smith returned, with a cunning smile:

вАЬHe has too much sense to come.вАЭ

They looked at him inquiringly.

вАЬFor why,вАЭ the smith explained, вАЬshould he have to vote for the schoolвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ or go to loggerheads with the District Official? And he will never vote: fancy what he would have to pay! No, he is wise.вАЭ

вАЬBut youвБ†вАФare you with us, Michael, say?вАЭ Matthew pressed him, eager to know.

The smith wriggled like a worm trodden upon, but, being in a quandary, grumbled a word or two, and went over to speak to the miller, who had come round to the peasants, and was now talking to old Ploshka very loud, for the rest to hear.

вАЬMy advice is: Vote as the officials wish. A school there must be: the worst is better than none. The one you wish for, yeвАЩll not get: вАЩtis no use knocking your heads against a wall. WonвАЩt you vote?вБ†вАФThen they will not ask your leave.вАЭ

вАЬBut,вАЭ cried a bystander, вАЬwhat can they do, if we give no money?вАЭ

вАЬYou are foolish. TheyвАЩll take it. Will you refuse?вБ†вАФThey will sell even your last cow, and send you to prison for mutiny into the bargain. Is that clear?вБ†вАФFor,вАЭ he added, turning to the Lipka folk, вАЬye have to do now, not with the Squire, but with the head official: a man who is not to be trifled with!вБ†вАФI tell you, do as they bid, and thank God things are no worse!вАЭ

Such as held his views here chimed in; and old Ploshka, after musing for some time, said on a sudden:

вАЬYe say true; and Roch misleads and seduces our folk.вАЭ

To this, one of the Przylek husbandmen added with emphasis:

вАЬHe is with the Manor folk, and therefore stirs us up against the Government.вАЭ

An outcry arose against him on every side; but he, undaunted, went on as soon as they let him.

вАЬThose,вАЭ he said, looking sagely around him, вАЬthose are fools that help him. If anyone likes this not, let him come forward: IвАЩll call him a fool to his face. Such men know not that it hath been so from all time: the gentry rebel and drive our folk to ruin; but who has to pay, when payday comes? Why, we peasants! When the Cossacks are quartered in your villages, who will get the beatings? who will suffer and be sent to prison? Only we peasants! The gentlefolk will not move a finger for you; they will slink away and leave you in the lurch, the Judases!вБ†вАФand, moreover, they will feast the officials in their manors!вАЭ

вАЬHa! What is the people in their eyes that they should stir for them?вАЭ cried one; and another:

вАЬIf they could, serfdom would be restored tomorrow!вАЭ

вАЬGregory says,вАЭ the former speaker continued, вАЬвАКвАШlet them teach in Polish; or, if they will not, let us vote no schools and no money for them.вАЩвБ†вАФVery fine. But вАЩtis only a labourer who can say to his master: вАШI will not work,вАЩ throw an insult in his face, and yet escape a thrashing by running away. We farmer folk cannot flee, and must needs stay and take the beating. Therefore I say it will come cheaper for you to build the school than to resist the officials. True, they will not teach our language; but they will never make Russians of us for all that: we shall none of us pray to God or speak among ourselves save as we do now, even as our mothers taught us!

вАЬFinally, I repeat: Stand up for your own interests only! Let the nobles tear each other to pieces: вАЩtis no affair of ours. Let them bite and fight: these are no more our brethren than those. And a plague upon them all!вАЭ

Here he was shouted down by the crowd that pressed about him. In vain did the miller and a few others take his part. Those on GregoryвАЩs side came near using their fists, and things were looking very bad, when old Prychek cried: вАЬThe gendarmes are listening!вАЭ

This silenced them and gave the old man an occasion to hold forth in an angry tone:

вАЬOne very true thing he has said: we must look to our own interests!вБ†вАФBe quiet there! You have said your say, let others say theirs!вБ†вАФThese fellows bawl and bawl, and think themselves great men!вБ†вАФIf shrieking meant thinking, then every loud-mouthed brawler would have a better head than even our priest himself! You laugh at me; but I say to you: how was it that yearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ when our noblemen rebelled? Remember how they threw dust in our eyes, and swore that as soon as Poland existed, we should have our willвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ our own landsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and forestsвБ†вАФand everything. And they made promises and speeches, and every other man of us helped them; and what have we of it now?вБ†вАФYe may hearken to the nobles, if ye be fools; but I am too old a bird to be caught with chaff!вАЭ

вАЬSmite him on the mouth, that he may be still!вАЭ cried a voice.

He went on nevertheless: вАЬAnd now I am a noble, as much as any of them all: I have my rights, and none dares lay a finger on me!вАЭ

But his voice was drowned in a torrent of jeers that poured down on him from all sides.

вАЬYou swine that grunt about your delights, and are happy to have a sty and a full trough!вАЭ

вАЬOnce fatted, you shall feel the club on your skull, and the knife at your throat!вАЭ

вАЬDid not a gendarme flog him at last fair? And yet he prates about no one daring to touch him!вАЭ

вАЬA great noble he is, and most free to be eaten by lice!вАЭ

вАЬTruly the straw stuffed in his boots could teach as much wisdom as he!вАЭ

вАЬHe knows not to judge a fowlвАЩs worth, yet he comes here to enlighten us!вАЭ

The old man was foaming with rage, but only said:

вАЬYe scum of the land!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that cannot even respect grey hairs!вАЭ

вАЬWhat then? Must a grey mare be respected, for that she is grey?вАЭ

They roared with laughter at this; but presently their attention was diverted to the roof of the office, on to which the constable Joseph had climbed, and, holding to a chimney, was gazing into the distance.

вАЬJoseph!вАЭ they cried to him in a merry mood. вАЬShut your mouth, lest something fall into it!вАЭ For a flock of pigeons was wheeling above his head. But he only shouted with all his might:

вАЬHe is comingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ coming! Has passed the turning from Krylak!вАЭ

The assembly now gathered close round the building, and gazed quietly along the road that as yet lay empty.

The scrivener hastily donned his very best clothes; again the air rang with his wifeвАЩs outcries, and the clinking of plates, and the rumbling of displaced furniture, and the noise of many feet. In a short time, the Voyt too appeared on the scene: standing on the doorstep, red as a beetroot, perspiring, breathless, but adorned with his chain of office. Casting his eyes on the crowd around him, he shouted in fierce tones:

вАЬSilence, men! This Office is not a tavern.вАЭ

вАЬCome round here, Peter! IвАЩll tell you what!вАЭ Klemba cried to him.

вАЬThere is no Peter here! I am an official,вАЭ he answered loftily.

The words were taken up at once and made great fun of, till they shook with laughter; but, all at once, the Voyt cried solemnly:

вАЬMake way there! Way for the head of the District!вАЭ

A coach appeared on the road, jolting over the ruts and hollows, and pulling up in front of the office.

The head official raised his hand to his cap, the peasants took their hats off, and silence followed, while the Voyt and the scrivener darted forward to assist him from the coach, and the gendarmes stood erect at attention beside the doorway.

He alighted, divested himself of his white dust-coat, turned round to gaze at the assembled crowd, stroked his blond beard, assumed a severe look, and nodded his head. Then he entered the scrivenerвАЩs dwelling, into which the latter, bent like a hoop, ushered him.

The coach drove away, and the peasants thronged round the table that had been set up. They thought the meeting was now about to begin. But it was a very long while indeed before the head official showed himself: while from the scrivenerвАЩs apartments there came the noise of jingling glasses, and laughter, and certain fragrant scents that made the mouth water.

They were weary both of waiting and of the broiling heat, and many a one tried to slip away to the tavern. But this the Voyt would not have.

вАЬDo not go away!вБ†вАФWhosoever is absent shall be put down for a fine.вАЭ

This kept them back, but they uttered many an invective, as they looked impatiently towards the scrivenerвАЩs curtained windows.

вАЬThey are ashamed to be seen drinking!вАЭ

вАЬQuite right of them: it would only make us more thirsty that we have but our spittle to swallow!вАЭ

From the lockup, in the same building as the Office, now came the constable, dragging by a halter a large calf that resisted with might and main and, making a sudden rush at him, upset the man and set off at a run, tail in air in a cloud of dust.

вАЬStop thief! Stop thief!вАЭ they cried, laughing.

вАЬOh, the bold rascal, to break prison so, even lifting up his tail against my Lord the Voyt!вАЭ

They also aimed a good many jibes at the constable, who was not able to get the calf into the yard without the assistance of all the Soltyses present. They were not yet fully breathed after their hunt, when the Voyt ordered them all to cleanse the lockup thoroughly; he himself saw that the work was well done, and helped them a good deal, fearing lest the District Official should make a tour of inspection.

вАЬBut, Voyt dear! yeвАЩll have to burn incense there, or his nose will tell him who the prisoner was!вАЭ

вАЬHave no fear: after a few drams he will scent naught in the world.вАЭ

And other gibes were thrown at the Voyt, which he only received with clenched teeth and glaring eyes.вБ†вАФAt last, however, they had enough of sun, and hunger, and waitingвБ†вАФand could not even jest any longer. So, in spite of the VoytвАЩs objurgations, they all made tracks for the tavern and the trees, Gregory flinging these words at him:

вАЬYe may cry till nightfall: we are no dogs to follow you to heel!вАЭ

Saying this, and glad to be no longer under the gendarmesвАЩ inspection, he again went about amongst the people, reminding each man apart in what sense he should give his vote.

вАЬAnd,вАЭ he would wind up, вАЬfear ye not: right is on our side. As we vote, so things shall be; and what we will not have, no man can force upon us.вАЭ

They had, however, not yet begun to stretch themselves in the shade, or to eat a morsel, when each village was called by its Soltys, and the Voyt came roaring:

вАЬHereвАЩs the head!вБ†вАФCome quick!вБ†вАФWe are to begin now!вАЭ

вАЬThe smell of good things has wrought upon him,вАЭ they muttered in a bad temper, walking slowly towards the Office. вАЬWe are in no hurry; let him wait!вАЭ

Each Soltys stood at the head of his own village; but the Voyt was seated at the table, beside the scrivenerвАЩs assistant, who whistled to frighten the pigeons, that circled round above the roof in a white fluttering cloud.

One of the gendarmes suddenly stood at attention, and cried: вАЬSilence!вАЭ in Russian.

To their disappointment, however, no one came out but the scrivener, holding some papers in his hand, and edging himself to a seat behind the table. The Voyt then rang the bell, and said, majestically:

вАЬGood people! we open the meeting.вБ†вАФBe still there, ye men of Modlitsa!вБ†вАФOur secretary will read you things concerning this school: only hearken ye diligently, that ye may know all about it.вАЭ

Putting on his spectacles, the scrivener began to read, very slowly and distinctly.

After a short interval of breathless silence, someone exclaimed:

вАЬWhy, we understand naught!вАЭ

вАЬRead it in our tongue! We cannot make it out!вАЭ repeated many voices.

The gendarmes here began to fix a steady glare upon the people.

The scrivener looked very black, but went on with the document, translating it into Polish.

All now were still, listening to each word with the most concentrated attention. The scrivener continued deliberately:

вАЬWhereas it hath been decided to found a school in Lipka, the same being also for the use of Modlitsa, Przylek, Rzepki, and the neighbouring hamlets.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

The rescript then pointed out how great a benefit education was; how the Government was night and day only thinking of means to aid the progress and enlightenment of the people, and to defend it from all evil influencesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then passed on to reckon how much would be required for the ground, for the building itself, and (yearly) for the teacher: concluding with the estimate that they ought to vote a supplementary rate of twenty kopeks per acre.вБ†вАФHe paused, wiped his spectacles, and added as an observation of his own:

вАЬThe head of the District has assured me that, if ye vote the rate now, he will allow the building to commence this year, so that next year in autumn your children will go to school.вАЭ

When he ended thus, no one made any remark. Everyone reflected with heads bent as under the weight of this fresh burden. At last the Voyt said:

вАЬHave ye heard all that our secretary has read to you?вАЭ

вАЬWe have indeed! We are not deaf!вАЭ several voices replied.

вАЬThen whoso is against this plan, let him step forward and say so.вАЭ

No one, however, was so bold as to put himself forward first, or go beyond glances and nudges.

вАЬThen,вАЭ the Voyt proposed, вАЬlet us vote the rate directly, and go home.вАЭ

вАЬVery well,вАЭ the scrivener asked, with solemn formality. вАЬYe do all unanimously agree to this plan?вАЭ

вАЬNo! No!вАЭ vociferated Gregory, and about a score of others with him.

вАЬWe need no such schools! We will not have them! The taxes are heavy enough as it is!вБ†вАФNo!вАЭ and cries of opposition now resounded on every side, and ever more boldly.

At the sound, the head official came forth and stood in the doorway. At the sight, the tumult died away. Stroking his beard, he said, with much affability:

вАЬWell, good husbandmen, how goes it with you?вАЭ

вАЬThe better that your Honour asks us!вАЭ answered the foremost men, swaying to and fro under the pressure of those behind, pushing forward to hear the District Official. Now he, leaning against the doorpost, uttered some sentences in Russian; but their effect was impaired by constant hiccups.

The gendarmes started forward, crying to the people:

вАЬHats off! Hats off!вАЭ

A voice was thereupon heard abusing them roundly: вАЬGet out, ye vermin, and meddle not with our business.вАЭ

But the head official, though he had spoken very affably, concluded in Polish, and in a tone of command:

вАЬVote the rate, and at once, for I have no time to spare.вАЭ

And he looked on them with an ominous scowl. Fear seized upon them; they wavered, and low timorous whispers ran through their ranks.

вАЬAh, shall we vote?вБ†вАФSay, Ploshka, what are we to do?вБ†вАФWhereвАЩs Gregory?вБ†вАФThe head commands us to vote!вБ†вАФCome, then, brethren, let us do so!вАЭ

But the tumult swelled to a storm, when Grzela came forward, and declared fearlessly: вАЬFor such a school weвАЩll not vote half a kopek!вАЭ

вАЬWe will not! No, we will not!вАЭ a hundred voices repeated.

At this, the head official knitted his brows.

The Voyt was terror-struck, and the scrivenerвАЩs spectacles fell from his nose. But Gregory met the great manвАЩs glance without fear, and was about to speak further, when Ploshka, pushing forward, and louting very low, said humbly:

вАЬMay it please your Honour the District Official if I speak in our tongue, and think with our own thoughts.вБ†вАФAs to voting the school, we are willing; but twenty kopeks an acre seems to us very much. Times are hard just now, and money is short. And that is all.вАЭ

The head made no reply, and seemed plunged in thought, only nodding his head at times, and rubbing his eyes. Encouraged by these gestures, the Voyt spoke strongly in favour of the school, and those of his party likewise, the miller distinguishing himself amongst them, and scorning the interruptions of GregoryвАЩs partisans, until the latter grew angry and shouted: вАЬWe are pouring empty vessels into the void!вАЭ and availed himself of an opportunity to step forward and ask boldly:

вАЬWe would know what kind of school this new one is to be.вАЭ

вАЬLike all the others!вАЭ he said, opening his eyes very wide.

вАЬThat is the very sort we do not want. WeвАЩd vote even half a rouble an acre for a Polish school, but not a stiver for any other.вАЭ

вАЬThose schools are good for nothing!вАЭ cried one. вАЬMy children learned there for three years, and do not know their A.B.C.вАЭ

вАЬBe still, good folk, be still!вАЭ growled the head.

The sheep were getting lively, and the wolf was biding his time.

вАЬThose infernal talkers! they will talk the people to its ruin!вАЭ

And now every man was striving to speak louder than his neighbours, and the din became deafening, each one maintaining his own view. They had broken up into small groups, disputing with one another, and getting ever more and more excited, GregoryвАЩs party especially standing up most stubbornly against the school. It was to no purpose that the Voyt, the miller, and the others of that side went about explaining, beseeching, even threatening awful things that might come to pass: the greater part of the assembly had got quite out of hand, excited to exasperation, and talking themselves hoarse.

The District Official, who sat seemingly indifferent to the hubbub, conferred in whispers with the scrivener, and let them talk their fill; and when he judged they had enough of that senseless noise, he told the Voyt to ring the bell.

вАЬSilence there!вАЭ thundered the Soltyses of each village. вАЬSilence! and lend your ears.вАЭ

Then, before all was quite still, rose the voice of command:

вАЬThe school, look ye! has to be built. Obey, then, and do as ye are bidden.вАЭ

His tone was as hard and stern as could be; but they were no longer afraid, and Klemba answered him back on the spot.

вАЬWe force no one to walk on his head: let others likewise allow us to speak in our tongue, as God has given it us!вАЭ

вАЬHold your tongue!вАЭ shrieked the Voyt, ringing the bell to no purpose. вАЬPeace, you son of a dog!вАЭ

вАЬWhat I have said, I repeat: in our schools our language must be taught!вАЭ

вАЬKarpenko! Ivanoff!вАЭ the Voyt cried to the gendarmes who stood in the centre of the throng; but the peasants pressed round them directly, and they heard a whisper: вАЬLet but one of you touch one of usвБ†вАФwe are three hundredвБ†вАФye shall see!вАЭ

Then their ranks opened slowly to let them pass, and closed after them, surging round the head official, with the dull angry hum of a furious mob; catching their breaths, cursing low, and one or other of them every now and then uttering such words as these:

вАЬEvery creature has its own voice; we alone are forbidden our own!вАЭ

вАЬAlways commands, and naught except commands! Obey, and pay, and sweep the ground with your hat, you peasant!вАЭ

вАЬTheyвАЩll make us soon ask leaveвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ to go behind the barn!вАЭ

вАЬSo mighty a man, let him command swine to sing as nightingales!вАЭ Antek cried. They laughed, and he went on, greatly excited:

вАЬOr bid geese to low like cows! When they do, weвАЩll vote the school!вАЭ

вАЬThey tax us, we pay; they recruit us, we go; but beware of.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬHold your peace, Klemba!вБ†вАФHis Majesty the Czar himself has decreed in the clearest words that our schools and law courts are to use Polish! Yes, the Czar himself has decreed it: him shall we obey!вАЭ Antek vociferated.

вАЬWho are you?вАЭ the head official said to him, with eyes intently fixed upon his face.

вАЬWho am I?вБ†вАФIt stands there in black and white,вАЭ Antek replied boldly, pointing to the papers on the table, though he felt his heart throb as he did so. вАЬI am no magpieвАЩs dropping!вАЭ he added with bravado.

The head spoke to the scrivener, who after a while proclaimed the fact that Antek Boryna, not being yet cleared of a criminal charge, had no right to take any part in the Assembly of the Commune.

Antek flushed angrily, but, before he could utter a word, the District Official cried out to him: вАЬGet him out!вАЭ indicating him to the gendarmes with a significant look.

вАЬBoys, never vote this school! Right is on our side: have no fear!вАЭ Antek shouted indomitably.

And with slow steps he went out of the village, looking back at the gendarmes following him yet more slowly still, as a wolf might glare at a couple of curs.

But the incident had brought disorder into the meeting again. Each man seemed possessed of a devilвБ†вАФscreaming, cursing, quarrelling, threateningвБ†вАФno one knew why or wherefore!

Their invectives bore, not only on the school and Antek, but on indifferent and wholly irrelevant mattersвБ†вАФjust as if a sudden madness had seized upon them. Gregory and others of his party strove to calm them, but unavailingly: they were blind and deaf to everything, gobbling one at another furiously, like irritated turkeys in a poultry-yard.

At last, one of the Soltyses, seeing an empty barrel that stood under the eaves of the house, had the idea of beating upon it with his stick so frantically, so madly, and with such loud and hollow bombilation, that it partly brought them to their senses again.

Thereupon the head official, who was beside himself with rage, exclaimed: вАЬEnough of this prating! Silence! Silence, when I speak!вБ†вАФObey me.вБ†вАФVote the school.вАЭ

All were in a moment struck dumb with fear: a cold thrill went through them. They looked at one another, without dreaming of defying the man who stood there, grim and threatening before them, rolling savage eyes over the terrified multitude.

Again he sat down, while the Voyt and his party once more attempted to frighten the peasants into obedience.

вАЬVote for the school!вБ†вАФWe must!вАЭ

вАЬHave ye not heard? An ill thing is impending!вАЭ

Meanwhile, the scrivener read the list of names, and the cry, вАЬHere! Here!вАЭ was heard with incessant reiteration.

This done, the Voyt ordered those who were for the school to pass to the right and raise their hands.

A good many did so, but the bulk of the assembly would not budge.

The head official then, knitting his brows, ordered the votes to be taken by name, вАЬthat all might be done with strict justice.вАЭ

Gregory was dismayed on hearing this order. He was but too sure that the majority would weaken, and not venture to oppose the vote.

The polling took a long time, for the people were very numerous; but the result was given at last:

вАЬAyes, two hundred; noes, eighty.вАЭ

GregoryвАЩs party raised a great protest.

вАЬWe have been cheated!вБ†вАФVote again!вАЭ

вАЬI said, No! and they put my vote down for the school!вАЭ one man, soon followed by many others, declared persistently; and the more zealous proposed to tear up the papers and thus annul the voting.

A coach from the Manor then passed by good fortune outside the Office, and the people had to draw back willy-nilly. The District Official, having read the list, handed to him by a manservant, declared solemnly: вАЬIt is well; ye shall have a school in Lipka.вАЭ

No one spoke a word any more; they all stood gazing at him in silence.

He then, after signing a few papers, got into his coach and drove away.

They all bowed to the ground. He took no notice of them, even by a glance; but, having spoken a few words with the gendarmes, turned off to the Manor of Modlitsa by a side road.

Their eyes followed him in silence. At last, one of GregoryвАЩs men said:

вАЬThat lamb, so meek and mild, can show fangs that bite deeper than a wolfвАЩsвБ†вАФaye, and when we least expect it, trample us under his feet!вАЭ

вАЬHow could they govern at all, unless we were fools and they scared us?вАЭ

Gregory breathed hard, looked round, and whispered:

вАЬFor today, we have lost: it is hard; but the people have not yet learned how to resist.вАЭ

вАЬAnd that they will hardly learn, so long as everything can frighten them.вАЭ

вАЬMy God! what a man! He tramples even the laws underfoot.вАЭ

вАЬAye, they are for us, not him!вАЭ

Here a peasant from Przylek came complaining to Gregory.

вАЬI meant to vote for you; but behold! when he fixed his eyes on me, I could not speak a word, and the scrivener wrote down what he pleased.вАЭ

вАЬThere have been so many abuses that we might well make an appeal.вАЭ

вАЬCome all to the tavern!вАЭ cried Matthew. вАЬMay a brimstone thunderbolt smash them all!вАЭ Then, turning to the crowd, he shouted:

вАЬDo ye know, my men, that the head has forgotten to tell you one thing?вБ†вАФThat ye are a rabble of sheep and curs. Ye will be well paid for your obedience; but such idiots as ye are deserve to be flayed aliveвБ†вАФnot only fleeced.вАЭ

They answered him back, some even abusing him roundly; but their attention was then drawn off by a cart with a Jewish driver, and Yanek sitting in it.

Yanek was soon surrounded by a crowd, and Gregory told him what had occurred. Yanek listened, talked to them for a while, and then drove on.

The others repaired to the tavern, where, after a couple of glasses, Matthew roared out:

вАЬI tell you, the Voyt and the miller are to blame for everything!вАЭ

вАЬQuite true,вАЭ Ploshka chimed in; вАЬthey were all the time canvassing and pressing and bullying us!вАЭ

вАЬAnd the head official threatened us, just as if he knew all about Roch!вАЭ someone faltered.

вАЬIf he does not, he is sure to be told. We have informers amongst us!вАЭ

вАЬWhat,вАЭ Gregory inquired with an uneasy glance, вАЬwhat has become of those gendarmes?вАЭ

вАЬGone somewhere in the direction of Lipka.вАЭ

Gregory for a short time lounged about the tavern with the others, but presently he slipped out unnoticed by anyone, making for Lipka by a shortcut across the fields.
